Curriculum About
Unit 1

The Evolution of PMM

60 minutes

Learning Objectives

By the end of this unit, you will:

  • Understand why we believe enterprise marketing is being transformed, and explore what key analysts are saying
  • Understand the Pragmatic Institute's 37-activity PMM framework – you will see the everyday work products you deliver, and understand which PMM activities have the highest AI impact potential
  • Map these activities to the B2B buying funnel, and develop this learning framework around a new "agentic" funnel where both humans and agents are making buying decisions
  • Understand how to be the "10x PMM" (a 10 times more productive and impactful PMM) that our curriculum hopes to develop

Section 1: Our Point of View

~15 min

The Shift We're Seeing

We've been looking at AI training at SAP for several months now, and conversations have changed dramatically. When we started, the questions were about tools: "Which AI should I use?" "How do I write better prompts?" But now the questions are different: "How do I stay relevant?" "What should I be focusing on?"

The speed of change has surprised even us. Not long ago, we were debating whether AI could write passable first drafts. The recent emergence of powerful models (e.g., Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 and OpenAI's ChatGPT 5.4) has accelerated everything. Today, our teams must leverage AI as a working partner across the entire PMM workflow — from competitive research to positioning to launch planning. The question isn't whether AI can do parts of our job. It's which parts, and what that means for how we spend our time.

Our Point of View

The "10x PMM" isn't someone who uses AI tools to do the same job faster. It's someone who builds a co-working relationship with AI that fundamentally changes what's possible — adapting to a new funnel, a new buyer, and a new way of working.

This isn't just our opinion. The world's leading analysts are documenting the same shift.

What the Data Shows

McKinsey's research teams have been tracking marketing performance and witnessed the difference between teams that adopt AI versus laggards.

Their November 2025 "State of Marketing" survey revealed a widening divide. 72% of marketing leaders plan to increase budgets in 2026. But the top 6% of companies — the ones McKinsey calls "AI high performers" — are achieving 22% efficiency gains through new working methods. The rest are falling behind.

With regard to enterprise spending and recent headlines about IT disruption, Gartner forecasts $6.15 trillion in global IT spending for 2026 (up 10.8% YoY), with enterprise software growing 15.2% — the fastest segment. The money is flowing, but it's only going to companies that have adapted.

72%
Marketing leaders increasing budgets (McKinsey)
22%
Efficiency gains for top performers
15.2%
Enterprise software growth (Gartner)
$6.15T
Global IT spend forecast

What This Means for Us

Enterprise marketing isn't shrinking. It's being rewired with AI as the central power source. The teams that adapt their approach to work with AI across the full stack — from awareness through renewal — will capture the growth. The teams that keep doing 2020-era PMM will lose ground to competitors who've made the shift.

Section 2: PMM Activities (What We Do)

~10 min

The 37 Activities of Product Marketing

The Pragmatic Institute's framework has been one of the standard maps of PMM work for over 20 years. It organizes our job into categories we all recognize:

Category Activities Include
Market Intelligence Market research, competitive analysis, win/loss interviews, analyst relations
Product Strategy Roadmap input, requirements gathering, pricing strategy, positioning
Content & Messaging Messaging docs, content strategy, thought leadership, case studies
Sales Enablement Battlecards, sales training, demo support, RFP responses
Campaign & Demand Gen Launch planning, campaign execution, lead nurturing, events
Operational Budget management, tool administration, cross-functional coordination

This is a pretty common framework. What most teams are getting wrong is pointing AI at the tactics within these tasks and thinking that more AI enables fewer workers. So far, that thesis has not been validated. Instead, we will look at each of these activities and ask, "What's the AI impact potential here?" Not, "Will AI take this job?" But rather, "How will AI change what excellence looks like in this activity?"

Section 3: The AI Impact Matrix

~15 min

Where AI Changes the Game

We analyzed each of the 37 Pragmatic activities against three criteria:

  1. Can current AI execute this at 80%+ of human quality?
  2. What's the cost and speed differential?
  3. How does this change what "good" looks like?

Today, everything we do must be evaluated through the lens of AI capability. Whether your core skill is writing, coding, strategic thinking, or research, there are going to be areas where AI tools can exceed your skills. Let's look at some estimates of what AI does best – and what humans need to fundamentally drive:

AI Impact by Activity

Activity AI Impact % Level
First-draft content (blog, email, ad copy)94%HIGH IMPACT
Basic competitive research91%HIGH IMPACT
Template-driven sales enablement88%HIGH IMPACT
Standard RFP responses85%HIGH IMPACT
Market sizing / TAM analysis82%HIGH IMPACT
Social & campaign copy79%MODERATE
Win/loss interview synthesis75%MODERATE
Product launch checklists72%MODERATE
Analyst briefing prep65%MODERATE
Sales training content60%MODERATE
Demo scripting54%EVOLVING
Pricing research & benchmarking50%EVOLVING
Customer interview synthesis42%LOWER IMPACT
Competitive strategy & positioning28%LOWER IMPACT
Narrative architecture & storytelling18%HUMAN-LED
Category creation & market framing12%HUMAN-LED
Executive communication & influence9%HUMAN-LED

The Key Insight

The activities that consume most of our time are those where AI has the greatest impact. This isn't a threat — it's an opportunity. When AI handles the volume work, we get time back to focus on the strategic work that actually moves the needle: positioning, narrative, executive engagement.

The key to this entire curriculum is starting to think about shifting from "having AI do it" and using AI tools to co-author work that is unique and differentiated – and can power every stage of the marketing funnel and create quality pipeline.

The Two Modes of PMM Work

Production Work (AI-Accelerated) Strategic Work (Human-Led, AI-Augmented)
First-draft content
Blog posts, emails, ad copy, social		 Narrative architecture
Making technical products feel like obvious choices
Basic research
Competitive monitoring, market sizing		 Competitive strategy
Pattern recognition built from years of market immersion
Template enablement
Battlecards, RFP boilerplate		 Category creation
Naming new markets, building cultural authority
Production copy
Localization, versioning, format adaptation		 Executive communication
Influencing C-Suite with incomplete data
Standard analysis
TAM, market sizing, interview synthesis		 Strategic positioning
Defining how the market perceives you

The 10x PMM Model

This is where our curriculum's biggest thesis lands. The "10x PMM" isn't someone who uses AI to do the same tasks 10x faster. It's someone who builds a co-working relationship with AI that fundamentally expands what's possible.

In production work, AI handles the first draft, the initial research, and the template. You become the director and editor — faster, yes, but also with higher quality output because you're focused on judgment, not mechanics.

In strategic work, AI should augment your thinking — surfacing patterns you'd miss, stress-testing your positioning, simulating stakeholder objections. In this mode, you are still leading the work, but with better, validated inputs.

That's the model this curriculum teaches.

Section 4: The New Funnel

~15 min

How Our Buyers Move Today

The B2B buying journey hasn't changed in structure — our buyers still move from awareness to purchase to renewal. What's changed is HOW they move through each stage, and what that means for our work. Let's review the key changes based on the typical buyer funnel:

Stage 1: AWARENESS

"What solutions exist for my problem?"

What's changed: Our buyers will increasingly start with AI assistants and search tools rather than going directly to vendor websites. Our content needs to be discoverable and be summarized by these tools. Yes – buyers are still finding us on sap.com and asking Google and Gartner their opinion, but the world is changing fast and the way we develop content must adapt to it.

PMM ActivityAI ImpactOur Adaptation
Market Research & Analysis82%Focus on insight synthesis, not data gathering
Content Strategy79%Optimize for discoverability across channels
Thought Leadership65%Build authority through substance, not volume
Digital Presence75%Structured data, clear information architecture

Stage 2: CONSIDERATION

"Which vendors should I shortlist?"

What's changed: Our buyers get vendor comparisons from multiple sources before visiting our site. Our positioning needs to be clear enough that it comes through accurately when summarized. There are some key content generation skills we will teach, including how to write for two audiences: humans and agents.

Pragmatic ActivityAI ImpactOur Adaptation
Positioning & Messaging28%Must survive summarization — crisp, quotable
Competitive Differentiation28%Clear, verifiable differences
Analyst Relations65%Analysts shape discovery — invest here
Value Proposition42%Quantified proof points that can be cited

Stage 3: EVALUATION

"Does this solution actually work for us?"

What's changed: Increasingly, agents will be doing the homework when looking at vendors. They will develop a RFI/RFP, manage the initial evaluation, and create the shortlist. They will develop lists of capability gaps, and arm procurement teams with questions. Our own sales teams will need to navigate this step of the buyer journey by having access to the right content and tools, and our external champions need ammunition to build internal consensus.

Pragmatic ActivityAI ImpactOur Adaptation
Sales Enablement88%Structured, searchable — not just PDFs
Technical Content75%Comprehensive enough for deep questions
Demo Strategy54%Self-service options for technical validation
POC Support60%Clear success criteria and proof points

Stage 4: COMMERCIALIZATION

"What will this cost and how do we justify it?"

What's changed: Procurement teams have better tools for comparing pricing and terms. Our commercial model needs to be transparent and defensible. Moreover, the world of per-seat licenses and SaaS based models is giving way to consumption-based pricing. Big enterprise software companies (like us) are adapting to this new commercial model, and so are traditional enterprise software buyers. Our models will need to adapt to the way customer and partner agents will consume our services and data.

Pragmatic ActivityAI ImpactOur Adaptation
Pricing Strategy50%Transparent, logically structured
Packaging & Bundling50%Clear value per tier
Business Case Development75%Quantified, verifiable ROI claims
ROI Modeling82%Data-backed projections

Stage 5: DECISION

"Can we get the committee to approve this?"

What's changed: Buying committees are larger and more informed. Each stakeholder does their own research. We need content that addresses each persona's specific concerns. AI can help us develop content and journeys for very specific buyer personas, and navigate the complexity of a multi-stakeholder buying decision.

Pragmatic ActivityAI ImpactOur Adaptation
Buyer Personas42%Include what each will research independently
Sales Plays72%Dynamic guidance, not static playbooks
Objection Handling65%Address concerns proactively
Executive Engagement9%Human-led — judgment remains critical

Stage 6: ADOPTION & EXPANSION

"Should we renew? Should we expand?"

What's changed: Our customers can easily benchmark us against alternatives throughout the relationship. We need to continuously demonstrate value, not wait for renewal conversations.

Pragmatic ActivityAI ImpactOur Adaptation
Customer Marketing72%Continuous value demonstration
Adoption & Onboarding75%Proactive success measurement
Expansion Strategy60%Signal detection for opportunities
Reference & Advocacy42%Generate discoverable proof points

Section 5: What This Curriculum Teaches

~5 min

Six Competencies for the 10x PMM

Based on this analysis, our curriculum develops six competencies — each mapped to a funnel stage and focused on the human-led, high-value work that AI augments rather than replaces.

Unit Title Funnel Stage Focus
Unit 2Creating Awareness through AI DiscoverabilityAwarenessStructured content, authority building, GEO
Unit 3Building Positioning that Survives SummarizationConsiderationCrisp differentiation, competitive clarity
Unit 4Enabling Champions for Technical ValidationEvaluationAI-ready technical content, self-service proof
Unit 5Designing Commercial Strategy for ScrutinyCommercializationTransparent pricing, defensible business cases
Unit 6Navigating Multi-Stakeholder DecisionsDecisionPersona-specific content, executive engagement
Unit 7Driving Adoption, Expansion, and AdvocacyAdoptionProactive value surfacing, renewal confidence

Why This Matters for Our Team

SAP has one of the strongest positions in enterprise software — decades of business data across finance, supply chain, HR, and customer experience. That's a foundation most of our competitors can't match.

However, capturing the opportunity requires product marketing that matches the moment. The playbook that worked five years ago (or even last month) won't work today.

This curriculum will teach the skills that matter now:

  • How to be discovered when our buyers research outside our website
  • How to position so our differentiation comes through clearly
  • How to enable champions who do deep technical homework
  • How to navigate buying committees where everyone arrives informed
  • How to drive renewals and expansion before customers look elsewhere
